required.title: [Reference]
required.author: Stephen Toulmin, Richard Rieke, and Allan Janik

suggested.title: [Watch] National Speech & Debate Association (NSDA) exemplar debates

competence_markers.description: The student can conduct a Socratic dialogue: asking clarifying, probing, and implication-testing questions in pursuit of truth rather than victory
The student can participate in a formal Lincoln-Douglas debate: constructive speech, cross-examination, rebuttal, and closing
The student can cross-examine an opponent effectively — asking targeted questions that expose specific weaknesses
The student can rebut arguments they have not heard before with coherent, structured counter-arguments
The student can argue a position they personally disagree with, constructing the strongest possible case for the other side
The student has demonstrated willingness to change their mind during or after a debate when the evidence warranted it
The student can analyze a recorded debate, identifying effective and ineffective cross-examination, rebuttal strategies, and argument construction
The student maintains a "changed mind journal" documenting positions they have revised and the reasoning behind the revision

narration_prompts.detail: How does it work? What is the difference between using Socratic questions to seek truth and using them as a weapon?

narration_prompts.prompt: Walk me through a formal debate structure.
narration_prompts.detail: What happens in each segment? What is the purpose of cross-examination? What makes a good rebuttal?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a debate where you argued a position you disagreed with.
narration_prompts.detail: What did you learn about the other side? Did it change your view at all?

narration_prompts.prompt: Describe a cross-examination you conducted or experienced.
narration_prompts.detail: What questions were most effective? Why? What makes a good cross-examination question?

narration_prompts.prompt: Have you changed your mind about anything as a result of a debate or dialogue?
narration_prompts.detail: What was the argument that changed it? How did it feel?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between dialectic and debate?
narration_prompts.detail: When is each appropriate? Can you do both at the same time?

cross_connections.description: B1 provides the construction toolkit (Toulmin model, three appeals, devices). B2 provides the interactive testing ground where those tools are deployed against a live opponent. Together they produce the complete argumentative capability.
Debate is public speaking under competitive pressure. The delivery skills from 3.3.6 — structure, impromptu response, vocal delivery, composure — are all deployed in debate.

Dialectic and debate sharpen the critical thinking that 1.7 develops defensively. The student who has been cross-examined and has cross-examined others has a lived experience of argument testing that purely defensive analysis cannot provide.

Cross-examination requires precise listening. Rebuttal requires hearing what the opponent actually said, not what you wished they said. Active listening is the foundation of every interactive debate skill.

Socratic dialectic is a philosophical method. The questions posed in dialectic are often philosophical questions — about justice, truth, the good life, the nature of knowledge. 3.3.9-B2 and 1.4 are natural allies.

Arguing with integrity — steelmanning the opposition, changing your mind when warranted, refusing to win through deception — requires the character foundation from IC1. Debate without character produces sophists. Debate with character produces honest advocates.

content.why_this_matters:
In 3.3.9-B1, you learned how rhetoric works — the three appeals, argument structure, rhetorical devices, speech analysis. That was the construction toolkit. Now you learn how to use it under pressure, in real time, against a living opponent who is trying to take your argument apart.

**Dialectic** is the art of finding truth through structured conversation. It was invented (or at least perfected) by Socrates, who believed that the best way to discover what is true is not to lecture but to question — to push and probe and follow the logic wherever it leads, even if it leads somewhere uncomfortable.

**Debate** is the competitive application of rhetoric: you construct a case, present it under time constraints, face cross-examination from an opponent who is looking for every weakness, rebut arguments you have never heard before, and deliver closing arguments that synthesize the entire exchange into a compelling final appeal.

Together, dialectic and debate develop something that no other training can: the ability to think under adversarial pressure. The student who has been cross-examined, who has faced questions designed to expose the weaknesses in their reasoning, who has argued positions they disagree with, and who has changed their mind in public when the evidence demanded it — that student has an intellectual and social capability that is rare and powerful.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Socratic method** — the art of questioning to expose assumptions, test consistency, and pursue deeper understanding
2. **Formal debate structure** — constructive speeches, cross-examination, rebuttals, and closing arguments in Lincoln-Douglas and simplified Parliamentary formats
3. **Cross-examination** — asking questions under time pressure that expose weaknesses in an opponent's argument
4. **Rebuttal** — responding to arguments you have never heard before with coherent, structured counter-arguments
5. **Arguing the other side** — constructing the strongest possible case for positions you personally disagree with
6. **The discipline of changing your mind** — recognizing when the evidence and argument warrant a position change, and doing so without shame
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Socratic Method — Thinking Through Questions

Socrates did not teach by telling people what to think. He taught by asking questions — questions so precisely aimed that they forced the other person to examine their own beliefs and discover the contradictions hiding inside them.

**The Socratic questioning sequence:**

1. **Clarify the claim.** "What do you mean when you say [X]?" — Most disagreements begin with people using the same words to mean different things. Clarification prevents arguing past each other.

2. **Probe the evidence.** "What evidence supports that?" "How do you know that is true?" "Where did you learn that?" — Many beliefs are held without evidence. The question exposes the gap.

3. **Test for consistency.** "You said X is true. But you also believe Y. Can both be true at the same time?" — People often hold contradictory beliefs without realizing it. The Socratic question surfaces the contradiction.

4. **Explore implications.** "If that is true, what follows?" "What would be the consequences of that position?" — Many positions seem reasonable until their implications are examined.

5. **Consider alternatives.** "Could there be another explanation?" "What if the opposite were true?" — Breaking open the assumption that the first explanation is the only explanation.

6. **Reflect on the process.** "What have we discovered? Has your view changed? Where are you less certain than when we started?" — The Socratic method is not about winning. It is about both parties arriving at a clearer understanding of the truth.

**The critical principle:** Socratic questioning is not a weapon. It is a truth-seeking method. The person asking the questions must be genuinely open to discovering that they are wrong — not just trying to prove the other person wrong. If you use Socratic questions as a trap rather than an inquiry, you are doing sophistry, not dialectic.

### Part 2: Formal Debate Structure

**Lincoln-Douglas Debate (Simplified)**

Lincoln-Douglas is a one-on-one debate format focused on values and philosophical arguments. Here is a simplified structure:

| Segment | Speaker | Time |
|---------|---------|------|
| Affirmative Constructive | AFF | 4 min |
| Cross-Examination | NEG questions AFF | 2 min |
| Negative Constructive + Rebuttal | NEG | 5 min |
| Cross-Examination | AFF questions NEG | 2 min |
| Affirmative Rebuttal | AFF | 3 min |
| Negative Rebuttal | NEG | 3 min |
| Affirmative Closing | AFF | 2 min |

**The Constructive Speech:** This is where you build your case. State your thesis. Define key terms (this matters more than you think — many debates are won or lost on definitions). Present your arguments in order, each with evidence and warrant. Anticipate your opponent's strongest argument and address it preemptively if time allows.

**Cross-Examination:** This is where you test your opponent's case through questions. The rules:
- Ask questions. Do not make speeches disguised as questions.
- Target the weakest point in the opponent's argument.
- Force the opponent to commit to specific claims that you can attack later.
- Listen to the answers. Do not ask the next question on your list if the answer to the current question opened a more productive line of questioning.

**Good cross-examination questions:**
- "You said [specific claim]. What is your evidence for that?"
- "If [your claim] is true, how do you explain [contradicting evidence]?"
- "You defined [term] as [definition]. Would you agree that [alternative definition] is also valid? And if so, does your argument still hold?"

**Bad cross-examination:**
- "Don't you think you're wrong?" (Not a real question.)
- Asking five questions in a row without letting the opponent answer. (You are speechmaking, not examining.)
- Asking about things not in the opponent's case. (Irrelevant.)

**Rebuttal:** This is where you respond to your opponent's arguments. The structure:
1. Identify their strongest argument.
2. State it fairly (do not straw-man).
3. Attack it with evidence, logic, or by exposing a fallacy.
4. Redirect to your own strongest argument.
5. Explain why, after hearing both sides, the judge should vote for you.

### Part 3: Arguing the Other Side

This is the exercise that makes debate transformative rather than merely competitive. You must argue positions you disagree with.

Why? Because arguing the other side teaches you something that nothing else can: genuine understanding of a position from the inside. When you have to construct the strongest possible case for a view you oppose, you discover the reasons — often good reasons — that lead intelligent people to hold that view. You stop seeing the other side as stupid and start seeing it as informed by different evidence, different values, or different priorities.

**The practice:**
- Choose a topic you have a strong opinion about.
- Construct the strongest possible case for the other side — not a weak version, but the best argument an honest, intelligent person on that side could make.
- Deliver that argument with conviction, as if you believed it.
- Then, and only then, construct your own case — which will be stronger because you have genuinely engaged with the opposition.

This is related to what 3.3.9-C will call **steelmanning** — the practice of building the strongest version of an opponent's argument rather than the weakest (straw-manning). It begins here, in the practice of arguing the side you disagree with.

### Part 4: Cross-Examination as an Art

Cross-examination is the highest-leverage debate skill. A great cross-examiner can dismantle a case in two minutes through questions alone.

**Principles of effective cross-examination:**

1. **Know what you are looking for.** Before you ask a single question, know the answer you want to elicit. Cross-examination is not exploration — it is targeted.

2. **Ask short, closed questions.** "You cited a 2019 study, correct?" "And that study had a sample size of 50?" "Would you consider 50 people a large enough sample to generalize about a nation of 330 million?" Each question is a small step. The destination is where the argument breaks.

3. **Do not argue.** If the opponent gives a bad answer, do not debate them. Just ask the next question. The cross-examination is building a record that you will use in your rebuttal.

4. **Control the pace.** You are asking the questions. You set the rhythm. If the opponent tries to give speeches instead of answers, redirect: "I appreciate the context, but my question was specifically: did the study have a sample size of 50?"

5. **Know when to stop.** When you have gotten the concession or exposed the weakness, stop. Do not ask one question too many — the question that lets the opponent recover.

### Part 5: The Discipline of Changing Your Mind

Debate trains you to argue any position. Dialectic trains you to follow the truth wherever it leads. Sometimes, the truth leads to changing your mind.

This is not losing. It is intellectual integrity. The person who never changes their mind regardless of evidence is not strong — they are stubborn. The person who changes their mind when the evidence warrants it is not weak — they are honest.

**The practice:**
- After every debate, ask yourself: "Did my opponent make any point that I cannot answer? If so, what does that mean for my position?"
- After every Socratic dialogue, ask: "Am I more or less certain of my original position? Where am I less certain? Why?"
- The journal of changed minds: keep a record of positions you have changed and the arguments that changed them. Review it periodically. This is the record of your intellectual growth.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Socratic Dialogue Practice [Bi-Weekly]
The student and mentor (or peer) engage in a Socratic dialogue on a genuine question — not a predetermined conclusion. The question should be one where neither party is entirely sure of the answer. Follow the questioning sequence: clarify, probe evidence, test consistency, explore implications, consider alternatives, reflect. At least 15 minutes per session.

### Study 2: Formal Debate Practice [At Least Monthly]
The student participates in at least one structured debate per month using the Lincoln-Douglas format (simplified or full). If a debate club is available, participate in competitive debates. If not, the mentor or a peer serves as the opponent. The student argues both affirmative and negative positions on different occasions.

### Study 3: Cross-Examination Drills [Bi-Weekly]
Separate from full debates, the student practices cross-examination as an isolated skill. The mentor presents a short argument (2 minutes). The student has 2 minutes of cross-examination. The goal: expose a specific weakness through questions. Switch roles so the student experiences being cross-examined.

### Study 4: Argue the Other Side [Monthly]
The student selects a position they strongly hold, constructs the strongest possible case for the opposite position, and delivers it. The mentor evaluates: Is this genuinely the strongest case? Or has the student unconsciously weakened the opposing argument?

### Study 5: Debate Analysis [Monthly]
The student watches or reads a competitive debate (NSDA recordings, historical debates) and analyzes: Where was cross-examination effective? Where did a rebuttal land? Where was an argument dropped? What would they have done differently?

### Study 6: Changed Mind Journal [Ongoing]
The student maintains a record of positions they have changed and the arguments or evidence that prompted the change. Review quarterly with the mentor. Celebrate intellectual honesty.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Socratic Method
**Developing well:** The student asks clarifying, probing, and implication-testing questions that drive a conversation toward deeper understanding. They are genuinely open to discovering they are wrong. Their questions are precise, not fishing expeditions.

**Developing slowly:** The student asks vague questions ("Why do you think that?") rather than targeted ones. They use questions as weapons rather than truth-seeking tools. They become defensive when their own views are questioned.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling Socratic questioning yourself? Is the student experiencing dialogues where the outcome is genuinely uncertain?

### 2. Debate Performance
**Developing well:** The student can construct a case, deliver it under time constraints, cross-examine effectively, rebut arguments they have not heard before, and deliver a coherent closing. They can argue both sides.

**Developing slowly:** The student can construct a case but falls apart during cross-examination or rebuttal. They cannot argue positions they disagree with. They lose composure under pressure.

**Diagnostic questions:** How much practice has the student had? Debate competence requires volume — dozens of rounds, not a handful. Are you providing enough practice opportunities?

### 3. Cross-Examination Skill
**Developing well:** The student asks short, targeted questions that build toward exposing a specific weakness. They control the pace. They know when to stop.

**Developing slowly:** The student makes speeches disguised as questions, asks irrelevant questions, or does not know what concession they are seeking.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student practicing cross-examination as an isolated skill, not just as part of full debates? The drill format — short argument, two minutes of questions — is the fastest way to develop this.

### 4. Intellectual Honesty
**Developing well:** The student acknowledges strong opposing arguments, changes their mind when warranted, and maintains a "changed mind journal" with genuine entries.

**Developing slowly:** The student never concedes a point, treats changing their mind as losing, or argues positions they disagree with in a deliberately weak way to prove the other side wrong.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you celebrating intellectual honesty when you see it? Are you modeling it yourself?
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Debate as Performance Only
If the student treats debate as a game to be won rather than a discipline that develops thinking, the training fails its purpose. Winning debates with clever tricks teaches cleverness, not reasoning. Push for substance: evidence quality, logical rigor, genuine engagement with the opposing argument.

### Avoiding Cross-Examination Practice
Cross-examination is uncomfortable — it exposes weakness. The student may avoid it or treat it as a formality. Make it central. The student who dreads cross-examination has not practiced enough.

### Socratic Dialogue as Interrogation
If Socratic questioning becomes a tool for cornering the other person rather than for mutual truth-seeking, it has been corrupted. When you see this pattern, stop and address it: "Are you asking because you want to understand, or because you want to trap?"

### Not Enough Rounds
Debate skill is built through volume. A student who does one debate per quarter is barely practicing. Monthly is the minimum. Weekly is better if a club or partner is available.
